## Core terms and variables

The space-expansion chapter inherits the momentum vocabulary of Foundations. It does not reopen it.

The bosic momentum magnitude is $p$. Its directional components are $p_k$, where $k$ labels a chosen spatial direction. The vector notation $\vec p$ may be used when the full directed momentum is meant, but the channel equations use components. The core momentum package is

$$
M(p)=\sqrt{p_f^2+p^2},
$$

with $p_f$ the inherited fermic momentum scale. The notation $M(p)$ emphasizes that the baseline total momentum content is being read as a function of the bosic magnitude already established in the inertial relation.

The directional-positive split remains the ADMC split:

$$
p_{k^+}=M(p)+\frac12p_k,
$$

$$
p_{k^-}=M(p)-\frac12p_k.
$$

The inverse identities remain:

$$
M(p)=\frac{p_{k^+}+p_{k^-}}{2},
$$

and

$$
p_k=p_{k^+}-p_{k^-}.
$$

The expansion object is

$$
\chi(t),
$$

called **translation yield**. It gives the amount of displacement produced by directed bosic momentum at epoch $t$. It multiplies the translation map; it does not scale the momentum variables. The observation epoch is normally used as the normalization point, so

$$
\chi(t_o)=1
$$

unless a different normalization is explicitly stated.

The basic yield map is

$$
\dot x_k(t)=\chi(t)c\frac{p_k}{M(p)}.
$$

Here $c$ is kept explicit. It is the local translation scale used to convert the dimensionless ratio $p_k/M(p)$ into a displacement rate. The epoch dependence belongs to $\chi(t)$, not to a variable-speed-of-light ontology.

For lightlike propagation, $p_k/M(p)=1$ along the propagation direction, so the map gives

$$
\dot x=\chi(t)c.
$$

The varying object is not the local light scale $c$, but the translation yield by which lightlike momentum becomes accumulated displacement and signal interval across cosmological propagation.

Two event labels will appear throughout the chapter:

$$
\chi_e=\chi(t_e),
\qquad
\chi_o=\chi(t_o),
$$

where $t_e$ is the emission epoch and $t_o$ is the observation epoch. The internal redshift mapping is written

$$
1+z=\frac{\chi_e}{\chi_o}.
$$

A useful yield-change scalar is

$$
H_\chi(t)=-\frac{\dot\chi(t)}{\chi(t)}.
$$

It plays a Hubble-like interface role for a translation-yield history. It is not automatically the full observational Hubble function, because a complete model still needs a propagation kernel, distance definitions, flux rules, and matter-sector dynamics.

The notation inventory for this chapter is therefore compact:

- $p$ — bosic momentum magnitude;
- $p_k$ — directional component of $\vec p$;
- $p_f$ — inherited fermic momentum scale;
- $M(p)$ — core momentum package in the baseline being read;
- $p_{k^+}$ and $p_{k^-}$ — positive directional ADMC channels;
- $\chi(t)$ — translation yield;
- $\chi_e$ and $\chi_o$ — yield values at emission and observation;
- $H_\chi$ — fractional yield-change rate;
- $c$ — local translation scale kept explicit in the map.

Matter mobility is a separate object from signal yield. If a later matter-growth extension needs screened or environment-dependent massive-tracer mobility, a candidate notation is $\mu_m(a,k,\mathrm{env})$. The universal signal yield $\chi(t)$ remains reserved for displacement, wavelength, and duration comparisons.
